pair_convention.spot_explanation: GBP/USD at 1.3232 means 1 GBP buys 1.3232 USD. A rising pair means GBP is strengthening against USD (Cable up); a falling pair means GBP is weakening against USD (Cable down). GBP is the base currency and USD is the quote currency. The pair is commonly referred to as 'Cable'.
pair_convention.bearish_means: pair FALLS (GBP weakens vs USD / USD strengthens)

executive_summary: GBP/USD trades at 1.3232 (latest close 2026-06-20), having broken decisively lower out of its year-long 1.32–1.36 range in the wake of the 16-17 June FOMC, sitting at the 26th percentile of its 52-week range (high 1.38621, low 1.30112). Cable is down 1.67% over 1M, 2.43% over 3M and 2.91% over the past year, with the entire recent move concentrated in the three sessions after a hawkish Fed under new Chair Kevin Warsh recast the 2026 US path from cuts to a likely hike. The dominant bilateral narrative is a rare both-hawkish central-bank standoff in which the dollar is winning: the BoE held at 3.75% on a more-hawkish 7-2 vote (both dissenters wanting a hike), yet the GBP-USD policy gap is a negligible ~+13bps, so sterling's own hawkish drift is being overwhelmed by a Fed that removed forward guidance and lifted its dot to 3.8%. This is the critical bilateral insight that neither standalone view fully captures: GBP is fundamentally supported on a carry/disinflation basis, but because that support is replicated and exceeded on the USD side, the pair signal is a clean USD-dominance trade rather than a GBP-weakness trade. The energy collapse (Brent sub-$80) is disinflationary and terms-of-trade-positive for the UK, but it is equally disinflationary for the US, neutralising the channel bilaterally. Our headline stance is tactically Bearish GBP/USD with moderate conviction on the hawkish-Fed momentum and the fresh range break, shifting to Neutral with a bearish tilt strategically as both central banks' carry edges fade, the US twin-deficit/valuation gravity reasserts, and the UK Autumn Budget tail looms — a genuinely two-sided 1-6 month picture where the cleanest GBP expression remains the dollar-stripped EUR/GBP cross.

market_context.valuation_assessment: On a real trade-weighted basis the pair pits two both-rich currencies against each other, muting the relative valuation signal. The GBP REER (111.49, ~82nd percentile) is modestly richer than the USD REER (107.06, ~78th percentile), implying GBP is marginally more overvalued than USD on its own history — a mild structural headwind for Cable. The base dossier flags GBP as roughly 5% cheap to USD-PPP (OECD ~1.41), but this is the residual post-Brexit discount rather than a mean-reversion catalyst, and with both currencies expensive there is no valuation tailwind to drive Cable in either direction. The net read: valuation neither supports nor opposes a directional Cable view materially, and any GBP-rich/USD-rich offset is a wash that leaves rate and momentum factors in control.

market_context.recent_regime: The pair's recent behaviour is defined by the collapse of the dollar-stripped narrative. Through April-May, Cable ground sideways in 1.34-1.36 as the post-war energy relief and UK disinflation offset a firming dollar. A stark divergence in monetary policy — with the Fed appearing more committed to fighting inflation than the BoE — drove the dollar higher and sent Cable to its lows on 17-18 June. The dominant narrative is now 'both hawkish, dollar wins' — a regime where GBP's own firm carry is real but bilaterally irrelevant against a more aggressive Fed, with nine FOMC members indicating one or more 2026 hikes and only one predicting a cut.

bilateral_carry_analysis.breakeven_move: At +13bps, spot need only move ~0.02% against a long-GBP position over a quarter to erase the carry — effectively zero protection
bilateral_carry_analysis.carry_assessment: The defining bilateral fact for Cable is that carry is essentially dead as a driver. With BoE Bank Rate at 3.75% versus the Fed's 3.625% mid, GBP earns a marginal +13bps annualised carry over USD — trivially positive and commercially irrelevant. The carry-to-vol ratio of ~0.018 provides negligible cushion; a long-GBP/USD position earns approximately 3 Cable pips per quarter from carry, an immaterial amount. This contrasts sharply with EUR/GBP (+150bps GBP carry) or GBP/JPY (+275bps), where carry genuinely funds the position. The forward curve confirms the dead-carry regime: GBP/USD forwards sit only fractionally above spot (+3 pips 1M, +9 pips 3M, +18 pips 6M), pricing near-zero GBP appreciation. With carry neutralised, Cable's direction is governed almost entirely by the relative rate-path trajectory and broad-dollar momentum — both of which currently favour the dollar and thus a Bearish GBP/USD stance.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The relative-rate channel is the decisive tactical driver and it points GBP/USD lower. Both central banks are hawkish-on-hold, but the trajectory diverges sharply: the Fed's 16-17 June dot lifted the end-2026 median to 3.8% (implying a likely hike), while the BoE held at 3.75% with two dissenting hikers — a hawkish vote, but one that markets read as defensive rather than imminently tightening. The GBP-USD policy differential is a negligible +13bps, so sterling's carry edge provides no buffer; the move is governed by which central bank is repricing toward hikes faster, and that is unambiguously the Fed. Warsh's determination to bring inflation back to target, combined with the removal of the statement of intent, pushed the dollar index to a 2.5-month high; he managed to unite the FOMC, with four dissenting voices at the previous meeting reduced to none at the June meeting. The front-end repricing is mechanical: the UK has undergone a dramatic policy U-turn from expecting cuts at the start of the year to debating hikes, giving the pound a hawkish tail risk that did not exist months ago — yet that same hawkish-tail dynamic exists on the USD side and is stronger, so the bilateral net favours the dollar. The 10Y gilt-UST spread (+28bps in GBP's favour) is a mild Cable support, but it is dwarfed by the front-end Fed repricing and broad-dollar momentum.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically the rate factor neutralises because both central banks' hawkishness is set to fade on the same energy-led disinflation. The post-war oil collapse should drag both UK and US headline CPI toward target over 1-6 months, eroding the rationale for tightening on both sides symmetrically — which leaves the +13bps gap roughly intact and the pair directionless on rates. The BoE's hawkish stance has softened somewhat, but the central bank is still expected to deliver 50bps in the back half of 2026. That Scotiabank view — that the BoE eventually cuts 50bps — is GBP-negative if realised, but consensus equally expects the Fed to revert to a 2027 cutting path, so the bilateral trajectory stays balanced. The relative path of the Bank of England against the Fed is what drives this pair, and right now that argues for cable holding in a range rather than breaking out. The key strategic uncertainty is sequencing: if the Fed clings to its hike threat into Q3 while the BoE drifts dovish on UK growth weakness, Cable grinds lower; if the Fed pivots back toward cuts first (the consensus base case), the dollar softens and Cable recovers.

carry_analysis.summary: Carry is essentially dead as a Cable driver — GBP earns only +13bps annualised over USD, giving a carry-to-vol ratio of ~0.018 (negligible). The forward curve is near-flat (+3/+9/+18 pips for 1M/3M/6M), confirming the dead-carry regime. With carry neutralised, Cable's direction is governed by the relative rate-path trajectory and broad-dollar momentum, both of which currently favour the dollar.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The inflation-growth differential favours the dollar tactically because the US is running both hotter inflation (which justifies the hawkish Fed) and stronger growth than the UK. US headline CPI is 4.2% with core PCE 3.3%, versus UK headline 2.8% and core 2.6% — the US inflation overshoot is what licences the Fed's hike threat and thus the dollar bid, even though high US inflation is theoretically currency-debasing. On growth, the US runs ~2.6% YoY with Q2 GDPNow near 3%, while the UK manages ~1.1% YoY with April monthly GDP at -0.1%. This growth differential (~+1.5pp in the US's favour) is a structural Cable headwind: US exceptionalism supports the dollar regardless of the inflation composition. The UK faces a weaker starting position — flat GDP, softening labor market data, and a fiscal position that limits the government's ability to cushion the energy shock through tax cuts the way Australia has. The asymmetry in data sensitivity matters: a hot US CPI print reinforces the Fed-hike trade (Cable down), while UK data must surprise sharply hawkish to move the pair against the dollar tide.

tactical.what_is_priced: The market prices US exceptionalism and the hot-CPI-driven Fed hawkishness as dollar-positive, which Cable's slide reflects. The edge is that both economies face energy-led disinflation ahead, so the US inflation premium is cyclical, not structural.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically the inflation differential converges as cheaper energy drags both headline rates lower, neutralising the tactical US advantage. The US core-vs-trimmed-mean gap (3.3% vs 2.3%) signals the US overshoot is energy-concentrated and likely transient, while UK services inflation (3.7%) is the stickier underlying problem — meaning over 1-6 months UK inflation could prove more persistent than US, a mild GBP-supportive offset to the growth gap. But the UK's structural growth weakness (OBR 1.1%, chronic productivity problems, fiscal drag from a tax-raising Budget) is a durable Cable headwind that US exceptionalism, even if fading, exceeds. The net is genuinely balanced: the US loses its inflation-overshoot dollar premium as energy normalises, but retains a growth edge; the UK gains a relative inflation-stickiness point but carries a heavier growth and fiscal burden.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: Cable is a low-beta risk pair, which mutes this factor bilaterally. Both GBP and USD have ambiguous risk profiles — GBP is mildly risk-positive (a cyclical currency), while USD is the ultimate haven, so in a risk-on regime GBP should outperform USD (Cable up) as the haven bid drains. The post-war regime is firmly risk-on (VIX ~16, equity records), which should support Cable. But the bilateral complication is that the hawkish Fed simultaneously reasserted USD's right-side-of-the-smile (exceptionalism) appeal, offsetting the left-side haven unwind. The net effect: the risk-on backdrop that would normally lift Cable is being neutralised by a dollar that is bid on rates rather than haven flows. Unlike high-beta pairs (AUD/JPY), Cable's risk sensitivity is too low and too two-sided to generate a clear directional signal — the pair is being driven by rates, not risk.

tactical.what_is_priced: The market prices the risk-on shift, but it is being expressed via higher-beta pairs (AUD), not Cable. The edge is minimal: Cable's low risk beta means the risk-on backdrop neither helps nor hurts decisively while the dollar is rate-driven.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Over 1-6 months the risk factor remains a low-weight, two-sided wash for Cable. A durable risk-on regime would gradually drain USD's haven premium and modestly support GBP, but the more decisive Cable drivers are the rate path and the UK-specific fiscal tail. In a risk-off shock (equity correction, ceasefire breakdown, Fed-driven tightening), both legs move adversely for different reasons — USD gains its haven bid (Cable down) while GBP loses its cyclical appeal (also Cable down) — so risk-off is unambiguously Cable-negative, but risk-on is only mildly Cable-positive. This asymmetry tilts the strategic risk distribution slightly bearish for the pair, but the modest 0.05 weight keeps it Neutral.

factors.horizon_split: Both horizons Neutral and aligned: Cable's low, two-sided risk beta generates no clear directional signal on either timeframe, with a faint bearish asymmetry (risk-off hurts both legs adversely for the pair).

Specs hold a small GBP leveraged long (+22,312) and a structural GBP asset-manager short (-109,656) on the base leg, while the USD aggregate long (~60-65th percentile) is concentrated in short-JPY/short-CAD with long-GBP an offset. The portfolio channel adds a mild Cable-negative tilt: the wide gilt-Bund premium reflects UK-specific risk that could deter foreign gilt (and thus GBP) inflows.

Over 1-6 months the dominant flow swing is the portfolio channel — foreign appetite for gilts versus Treasuries — which the Autumn Budget and the gilt-Bund premium will govern. With both speculative books slow-moving and no extreme, positioning generates no strong directional pair signal.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The terms-of-trade channel from the energy collapse nets out bilaterally, which is the key insight versus the standalone GBP dossier (which scored this tactically Bullish for sterling in isolation). Using the commodity direction rules: oil down is mildly positive for GBP (net energy importer, narrower trade deficit) but the US is now a net energy exporter, so cheaper oil is a mild terms-of-trade negative for USD (lower export receipts) yet a consumer/inflation positive — roughly a wash on the USD side. The UK gains modestly more from cheaper energy than the US loses, giving a faint GBP-positive tilt, but the effect is small and slow-transmitting, and the bilateral trade relationship is near-balanced: per IMF DOTS, GBP exports to USD were $73.7bn vs GBP imports from USD of $72.7bn, with a UK net goods surplus versus the US of just $1.0bn — a negligible bilateral flow that generates no structural pair pressure. With both economies benefiting from cheaper energy on net (the UK via lower import costs, the US via lower consumer prices), the terms-of-trade signal is genuinely neutral for Cable tactically.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: At the heavy 0.16 strategic weight, the external-balance factor leans mildly Cable-bearish — a notable bilateral reversal from the GBP standalone read. Both economies run twin/structural deficits, but the comparison is closer than the GBP dossier's standalone bearishness implies: the UK current account (-2.4% GDP) is actually narrower than the US (-3.6% GDP), which in isolation favours GBP. However, the bilateral swing factor is financing vulnerability, and here the UK is more exposed: the UK relies on foreign gilt buyers (~25-30% of the stock) precisely when the gilt-Bund premium is wide and fiscal headroom is thin, whereas the US enjoys reserve-currency 'exorbitant privilege' that finances its larger deficit cheaply. So despite the US's wider headline deficit, the dollar's structural funding advantage and the UK's 'kindness of strangers' fragility tilt the pair mildly Cable-negative over the horizon. The energy terms-of-trade improvement helps the UK trade balance cyclically but does not fix the structural financing dependency.

net_commodity_exposure.base_commodity_profile: GBP/UK: net energy importer — cheaper oil and gas is terms-of-trade positive (lower import costs, narrower current account deficit). UK NBP gas ~99p/therm (-20% MoM); Brent sub-$80 (-30% MoM).
net_commodity_exposure.quote_commodity_profile: USD/US: net energy exporter since shale revolution — cheaper oil is a mild terms-of-trade negative for the US (lower export receipts) but a consumer/inflation positive (lower petrol prices support consumer spending and lower CPI).
net_commodity_exposure.net_effect_calculation: Step 1 — Oil direction: Brent sub-$80, falling. Step 2 — GBP leg: UK is a net energy importer, so falling oil is mildly GBP-positive (lower import costs, narrower trade deficit, disinflationary relief). Step 3 — USD leg: US is a net energy exporter, so falling oil is mildly USD-negative on terms of trade (lower export receipts), but partially offset by the consumer and inflation positives (lower CPI supports real incomes, reduces pressure on the Fed to hike further — actually a mild USD-negative from the rate-path channel too). Step 4 — Net bilateral effect: GBP gains modestly more from cheaper energy than the US loses, giving a faint GBP-positive tilt for Cable. Step 5 — Magnitude check: the effect is small and slow-transmitting; the bilateral trade relationship is near-balanced ($1.0bn UK goods surplus vs US per IMF DOTS 2024). Step 6 — Conclusion: net faint GBP-positive from commodity channel, but too small to drive the pair near-term tactically; at the strategic 0.16 weight this energy-positive is outweighed by the UK's financing fragility.
net_commodity_exposure.scenario_oil_up_10pct: Oil +10%: mild GBP-negative (higher UK import costs, wider CA deficit, inflationary — may force BoE tighter but at cost to growth); mild USD-positive (higher US export receipts, improved US terms of trade). Net: modestly Cable-negative.
net_commodity_exposure.scenario_oil_down_10pct: Oil -10%: mild GBP-positive (lower UK import costs, narrower CA deficit, disinflationary); mild USD-negative on terms of trade (lower export receipts) but inflation-positive offset. Net: modestly Cable-positive but small in magnitude.
net_commodity_exposure.dominant_commodity_driver: Brent crude oil — the bilateral swing factor given the UK's net-importer status versus the US's net-exporter status. Gas (UK NBP) is the secondary driver given the UK's gas-import reliance.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: Both central banks delivered hawkish guidance in June, but the Fed's was the more forceful and market-moving, tilting the pair lower. The BoE's 7-2 hawkish-hold (two hikers) was a credible no-cut signal, but the Fed's removal of forward guidance, a unanimous 12-0 vote, and a dot lifted to 3.8% under a new, overtly anti-inflation chair was a regime-defining hawkish shift that dominated. The BoE's vote split turned more hawkish, with two members now backing a rate increase, but cable enters the long weekend pinned at a two-month low, caught between a newly hawkish Fed powering the dollar and a Bank of England that is itself leaning toward tightening. The bilateral guidance differential is 'both hawkish, Fed more so' — and crucially, Warsh's debut bought the Fed fresh anti-inflation credibility precisely as the BoE's hawkishness is seen as defensive. With both banks' guidance pointing the same hawkish way, the relative-credibility edge accrues to the more aggressive and newly-credible Fed, dragging Cable down.

tactical.what_is_priced: The market prices the Fed's hawkish guidance as the dominant force, which Cable's slide reflects. The edge is the 30 July MPR sequencing: a hawkish BoE MPR after the FOMC could provide a modest Cable bounce, but the Fed sets the broad-dollar tone.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically guidance is a higher-weight (0.12) but balanced factor, with a distinctive bilateral wildcard: Fed-independence risk. The BoE's transparent, scenario-based MPR framework is on a path to soften as energy disinflation broadens (GBP-negative via lost hawkishness), but the more asymmetric risk sits on the USD side — Warsh is a Trump appointee, and a politicised dovish pivot (easing under political pressure rather than data) would erode the dollar's credibility premium sharply (Cable-positive). This is the key bilateral guidance tension: both banks' current hawkishness fades over the horizon, but the dollar carries a larger credibility tail (independence) that could trigger an outsized USD-negative repricing. Conversely, if Warsh sustains his anti-inflation credibility while the BoE drifts dovish, the pair stays pressured. The two-sided, fat-tailed nature leaves it Neutral.

strategic.what_is_priced: Consensus prices both banks as credible and gradually less hawkish. The edge is the asymmetric Fed-independence tail — an under-priced USD-negative (Cable-positive) scenario that has no GBP-side equivalent.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The dominant 2026 tail — the Iran-US war — resolved favourably for both currencies, neutralising it as a bilateral pair driver. The ceasefire is risk-on (mild GBP+ via cyclical appeal, mild USD- via haven unwind) but the hawkish Fed offset the USD haven-unwind, so the de-escalation nets to roughly neutral for Cable. The near-term event calendar is data-heavy and symmetric: US Non-Farm Payrolls, released on the first Friday of each month, is the single biggest scheduled dollar mover, and US CPI, released mid-month, is the second — both can move the pair via the USD leg, while the 22 July UK CPI and 30 July BoE MPR drive the GBP leg. With no UK-specific binary in the tactical window (the Autumn Budget is months away) and the war resolved, the event factor is a balanced wash for Cable, with the ceasefire-breakdown tail (Brent re-spike) being ambiguous — risk-off would be Cable-negative via the USD haven.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically the event calendar skews mildly Cable-bearish because the UK-specific fiscal tail (the Autumn Budget) is a binary GBP-negative with no symmetric USD equivalent that is as acutely dated. The November Budget — with thin fiscal headroom (<£10bn), debt ~95-96% and a record tax burden — is the dominant strategic event, and a fragile, 2022-style outcome would trigger gilt stress and sharp GBP weakness (Cable down). On the USD side, the strategic calendar (mid-August Iran deadline, 1 October funding cliff, 3 November midterms) is genuinely two-sided. The 3 November US midterm elections affect control of Congress, which affects the fiscal outlook and the dollar. The asymmetry is that the UK Budget is a concentrated, predictable GBP-negative binary, while the US events are diffuse and two-sided — so on net the strategic event distribution tilts Cable lower, with the Budget the most likely range-breaker.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: Both sovereigns carry meaningful fiscal vulnerabilities, and neither has a dated tactical trigger, so the factor is a balanced wash for Cable near-term. The UK runs debt/GDP ~95-96% with thin headroom and a wide gilt-Bund premium (+183bps), while the US runs a larger deficit (~5.8% of GDP), debt/GDP ~101%, net interest $1tn, and lost its last AAA (Moody's Aa1, May 2025). On the level of fiscal stress the US is arguably worse, but the US enjoys reserve-currency financing that the UK lacks — so the bilateral fiscal comparison is closer than either standalone view. With no Budget, auction stress, or rating action dated in the window, and gilts actually easing as the war premium unwinds, the factor generates no tactical pair signal.

The UK's <£10bn headroom against a 2022-mini-budget precedent makes the Budget a genuine gilt-stress binary; the US fiscal trajectory, while structurally worse, is slow-burn and financed by the dollar's exorbitant privilege. The bilateral asymmetry: a UK fiscal slippage would trigger a fast, sharp GBP-negative gilt-market repricing, whereas US fiscal concerns erode the dollar only gradually and are partly offset by haven/reserve demand. So despite the US's larger deficit, the UK's concentrated, near-dated fiscal fragility makes the pair more vulnerable to a GBP-side shock — Cable-bearish at the 0.07 weight.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The pair's own technicals turned bearish on the 17 June range break. Spot (1.3232) sits below the tightly-clustered 50d/100d/200d MAs (all 1.341-1.345), a bearish alignment after the multi-month range finally cracked lower. From web-sourced visual levels, resistance is layered at ~1.3428, 1.3474 and 1.3522 (key), with support at 1.3302 (key, now broken), 1.3194 and 1.3092. On the topside, the primary hurdle is the May 26 high around 1.3500, followed by the descending resistance trend line with its break price near 1.3580. The variance risk premium (IV ~7.2% vs RV pre-break ~5.5%) suggests options carried an event premium that the break has begun to realise; the mildly negative risk reversal (GBP puts bid) on both Cable and the EUR/USD proxy means the market was pre-positioned for this downside break. With spot below all MAs and below 'key' support, the structure is decisively bearish unless 1.3428 is reclaimed.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically the structure is Neutral with a bearish tilt, subordinate to fundamentals. The broader picture remains constructively framed by some analysts — price action from 1.3867 is merely a corrective pattern within the broader uptrend from 1.0351, and with 1.3008 support intact, medium-term bullishness is maintained, with a break of 1.3867 favoured towards 1.4248 key resistance. So while the near-term break is bearish, the multi-year structure holds above the pivotal 1.3000/1.3008 zone. A strategic downside break below 1.3000 would invalidate the multi-year bull case and trigger CTA/trend-following selling; an upside reclaim above 1.3522-1.3580 would re-open the 1.36-1.38 range. The structure tells us the catalysts to watch (Budget, BoE/Fed paths) rather than providing its own signal, with the puts-bid skew tilting the more probable resolution to the downside.

expression_assessment.vol_landscape_summary: Cable's implied vol regime was compressed pre-break (~40th IV percentile, 1M IV 7.2%, RV ~5.5%, positive variance premium) but the 17 June range break has begun realising the event premium, with realised vol jumping and the variance premium compressing. Risk reversals are mildly negative (GBP puts bid ~-0.3 vols) on both Cable and the EUR/USD proxy, confirming the market was pre-positioned for the downside break.
expression_assessment.directional_expression: For the tactical-bearish view, spot expression (short Cable) or a modest put spread targeting 1.3194/1.3092 is cleanest now that the breakout has confirmed. The downside-skewed risk reversals (GBP puts bid) mean pure put-buying is relatively expensive; a put spread (buying 1.32 puts, selling 1.30 puts to cheapen) better expresses the targeted move toward the 52-week low while capping cost. For the strategic view, the negative expected value from an outright Cable short means optionality around the Autumn Budget is the correct expression — buying longer-dated GBP downside or a Budget-window strangle while vol is still moderate. The cleanest dollar-stripped GBP expression remains the EUR/GBP cross for those wanting to isolate UK fundamentals from the broad-dollar move dominating Cable.
expression_assessment.event_timing_consideration: The near-term expression must account for the dense data cluster: US core PCE (25 Jun), NFP (2 Jul), US CPI (14 Jul), UK CPI (22 Jul), FOMC (28-29 Jul) and BoE MPR (30 Jul). These events raise vol into July. The Autumn Budget (~Nov) is the dominant strategic expression window — building optionality into the Budget period while vol is moderate is the asymmetric play for the fiscal-tail risk.
expression_assessment.liquidity_note: Cable is the third most-traded currency pair globally — deeply liquid with tight spreads and orderly price action outside of specific shock events. Execution risk is minimal for spot and standard options; position sizing is not constrained by liquidity. The main execution consideration is the compressed variance premium post-break, which makes buying options less attractive than pre-break.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: This factor is the purest USD-leg driver and currently the dominant Cable-bearish force alongside rates. The broad trade-weighted dollar is firm (~119.5, DXY ~100.6), propelled by the hawkish Warsh Fed, and this broad-dollar strength sits directly on top of Cable regardless of UK fundamentals — it is precisely why GBP failed to rally on the oil relief and then broke lower on the Fed. The US Dollar Index climbed to a two-month high near 100.57 following the hawkish Fed, breaking above key resistance and trading above its rising trendline and major moving averages with momentum behind it. SOFR is stable (~3.63-3.69%, at IORB) with no funding dislocation, and QT has ended, so this is a firm-dollar-on-rates story, not a funding-stress story. Through the dollar-smile, GBP lacks a haven bid and sits on the wrong side of a dollar that is bid on both rate momentum and (latently) exceptionalism — an unambiguous Cable headwind tactically.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically the dollar-funding factor is two-sided for Cable. The dollar's near-term strength rests on the hawkish Fed, but the strategic dollar path is genuinely uncertain: if US disinflation resumes (helped by cheaper oil) and the Fed reverts to its 2027-cut path, the broad dollar softens and the cap on Cable lifts (GBP+); if US exceptionalism and Fed hawkishness persist, the dollar stays firm (GBP-). The upper end of each DXY range reflects the dollar staying firm; the lower end reflects the softer-dollar case playing out. Consensus leans toward eventual dollar softness (EUR/USD 1.20-1.25, supporting Cable recovery), but the hawkish Warsh stance could delay it. With no funding stress and QT over (mild USD-negative via removed liquidity drain), the factor nets Neutral — the dollar's strategic direction is a coin-flip that will determine whether Cable recovers toward 1.36+ or stays pinned near the lows.

tactical.score_interpretation: A net score of -0.46 sits firmly in Bearish territory. Mapping mechanically toward conviction 3-4; held at conviction 3 because the move is heavily concentrated in the two heavyweight factors (rates and inflation), much of the repricing has already occurred in the 17 June break, and the strategic picture is far less bearish — capping the tactical conviction.
tactical.rationale: The tactical view is Bearish GBP/USD with moderate conviction, and the -0.46 weighted score is dominated by the two largest-weight factors plus the USD-funding leg. Policy & Rates (0.24, -0.163) and Inflation & Growth (0.18, -0.108) together contribute -0.27 — the bulk of the bearish tilt — because the Fed's June regime change (a likely hike, not cuts) outpaced the BoE's defensive hawkishness, and the US runs both hotter inflation and stronger growth. The critical bilateral insight is that the two standalone dossiers conflict in a way that resolves bearishly: the GBP dossier is mildly bullish sterling on carry and disinflation, but every one of those supports is replicated and exceeded on the USD side, so the combined view is not 'GBP weak' but 'USD stronger' — a clean dollar-dominance trade. USD Liquidity (0.11, -0.064) reinforces this: the US Dollar Index climbed to a two-month high near 100.57 following the hawkish Fed, breaking above key resistance with momentum behind it. The factors conflict at the margins: External Balance, Event Risk and Fiscal are Neutral because the energy-relief and war-resolution channels net out bilaterally (both economies benefit), and Risk Appetite is Neutral because Cable's low beta is two-sided. Flows lean bearish on the post-break vulnerability of the GBP leveraged long. The path of least resistance is continued Cable downside toward the 52-week low: a stark divergence in monetary policy — with the Fed appearing more committed to fighting inflation — has driven the dollar higher against the pound and sent cable to its lows. What is priced is the Fed-over-BoE repricing; what we expect is some continuation toward 1.3092/1.3000 as the dollar momentum and bearish technicals carry, but with most of the repricing done. Key dated catalysts: 25 June core PCE, 2 July NFP, 14 July US CPI, 22 July UK CPI, 28-29 July FOMC, 30 July BoE MPR. The conviction driver is the alignment of rates, inflation, guidance, structure and dollar-funding all pointing Cable down; the conviction cap is that the move is concentrated, largely priced, and reverses sharply if a single soft US print or a hawkish BoE MPR surprises.
tactical.g10_relative_ranking: Among USD pairs, GBP/USD ranks as a middling short — the dollar is bid against nearly all G10, but Cable is a less attractive USD-long expression than USD/JPY (where carry, the +274bps 10Y gap and momentum align) or short-AUD/short-CAD plays. Among GBP crosses, GBP/USD is the weakest sterling cross precisely because the USD leg is the strongest counterpart — GBP outperforms EUR (firm carry), JPY and CHF, so a GBP bull should express via EUR/GBP or GBP/JPY, not Cable. With Gulf headlines dominating broader dollar direction, cleaner expressions of shifting UK rate pricing and domestic fundamentals may be more evident against the crosses. Net: Cable is a moderate USD-long but a poor GBP expression — the pair sits mid-pack among USD shorts and last among GBP crosses.

strategic.score_interpretation: A net score of -0.15 sits inside the Neutral band (-0.20 to +0.20) with a clear bearish tilt, mapping to conviction 2 — a low-conviction lean toward Cable softness, not a directional bearish call, because the rate, inflation, guidance and dollar-funding factors all neutralise strategically.
strategic.rationale: The strategic view is Neutral with a bearish tilt and low conviction; the -0.15 score reflects the disappearance of the tactical dollar-momentum tailwind once the horizon lengthens. The four heaviest factors all neutralise: Policy & Rates (0.24) because both central banks' hawkishness fades symmetrically on shared energy disinflation; Inflation & Growth (0.10) because the inflation gap converges as energy normalises; Guidance (0.12) because both banks soften but the Fed carries an asymmetric independence tail; and USD Liquidity (0.12) because the dollar's 1-6 month direction is a genuine coin-flip. With the heavyweights neutralised, the strategic score is carried by three structural negatives: External Balance (0.16, -0.086), Fiscal (0.07, -0.039) and Event Risk (0.05, -0.027) — all reflecting the UK's concentrated, near-dated fiscal/financing fragility (the November Budget, thin headroom, foreign-gilt reliance) versus the US's chronic-but-reserve-financed deterioration. The standalone dossiers reinforce here: the USD dossier is strategically Neutral-bearish (twin deficits, rich REER), while the GBP dossier is strategically Neutral with a fiscal shadow — combined, the pair leans mildly Cable-lower on the UK's sharper fiscal binary. The relative path of the Bank of England against the Fed is what drives this pair, and right now that argues for cable holding in a range rather than breaking out. What is priced is the consensus 1.33-1.40 range with eventual dollar softness supporting a Cable recovery; what we expect is similar but with the UK Budget as a fatter downside binary than consensus point-forecasts imply. The path of least resistance is range-bound-to-softer with the November Budget the decisive inflection. Key strategic catalysts: 30 July and 5 November BoE MPRs, the Autumn Budget (~Nov), the 28-29 July / 15-16 Sept (SEP) / 9 Dec FOMCs, the mid-August Iran deadline and the 3 November midterms. Conviction is 2 because the offsetting forces are genuinely balanced and the view is close to consensus — best expressed as a relative-value lean (short EUR/GBP for GBP-bulls, or a Cable Budget-hedge) rather than a directional Cable position.
strategic.g10_relative_ranking: Strategically Cable sits mid-pack among USD pairs and at the bottom among GBP crosses. Both currencies rank ~6/11 in their respective standalone strategic tables — a symmetric mid-table standoff that confirms the pair's Neutral read. Among USD pairs, Cable is a weaker USD-long than the high-yield commodity shorts (AUD, NOK) because the GBP leg retains firm carry — so the dollar bull prefers other pairs. The cross-pair signal is clear: use EUR/GBP to express GBP views, and use AUD/USD or USD/JPY to express USD views.

consensus_divergence.our_strategic_view: Neutral with bearish tilt — broadly consensus-aligned range-bound recovery toward 1.34-1.36, but with a fatter downside Budget binary than consensus point-forecasts imply
consensus_divergence.divergence_direction: Tactically below consensus; strategically in-line with downside skew
consensus_divergence.divergence_magnitude: Tactically ~300-400 pips below consensus 2026 year-end targets; strategically roughly in-line with Goldman but below MS/MUFG
consensus_divergence.why_we_differ: We differ from consensus in two ways. Tactically, most bullish forecasts predate the June Fed regime change and assume Fed cuts that are no longer the base case — we see Cable pressured toward 1.30-1.32 near-term while the hawkish Warsh Fed dominates. We also recognise the bilateral dead-carry reality: GBP's +13bps edge gives no buffer, leaving Cable a pure dollar trade. Strategically, we weight the Autumn Budget's downside binary more heavily than a stable point-forecast implies — the UK's <£10bn headroom, 95-96% debt/GDP, and foreign-gilt reliance create a concentrated fiscal tail that consensus 1.36-1.40 forecasts systematically underweight. The bilateral financing-asymmetry insight (UK 'kindness of strangers' fragility vs US reserve-currency privilege) is the key factor we weigh differently from the street.
consensus_divergence.payoff_if_right: Being short Cable (or holding GBP downside) into Q3 against a Street positioned for 1.36-1.40 captures 200-400 pips if Cable holds 1.30-1.32
consensus_divergence.risk_if_wrong: If oil-driven disinflation arrives fast and the Fed reverts to cuts, the consensus recovery materialises quickly and the firmer-dollar near-term call is stopped out

cross_horizon_consistency.explanation: Tactical and strategic diverge in stance but are mechanistically consistent — both reflect the same 'both-hawkish, dollar-momentum-now/structural-balance-later' tension over different windows. Tactically the live Fed-over-BoE repricing and firm-dollar momentum drag Cable lower; strategically those same drivers neutralise as both central banks' hawkishness fades symmetrically and the dollar's direction becomes a coin-flip, leaving the UK fiscal tail as the residual mild negative.
cross_horizon_consistency.inflection_point: The September Fed SEP (15-16 September) and the broadening of energy disinflation through the autumn CPI prints — the key checkpoint where the Fed either sustains or abandons its hike threat
cross_horizon_consistency.trading_implication: Run the tactical Cable short with a tight leash and a defined exit around the late-July/September data cluster, then rotate toward the strategic Neutral stance — covering the short and expressing any residual GBP view via short EUR/GBP (dollar-stripped) or a Budget-window hedge. Avoid an outright strategic Cable short given the negative expected value from the consensus recovery base case.

forward_vs_spot.forward_1m: ~+3 pips → outright ~1.3235 (annualised ~+0.3%, reflecting the +13bps GBP-USD carry)
forward_vs_spot.edge_3m: -40 pips (forward implies 1.3241, we expect ~1.320 — the short is favoured at 3M)
forward_vs_spot.edge_6m: +100 pips (we expect ~1.350 strategic recovery vs forward 1.3250 — the long is favoured at 6M)

catalysts.event: US Core PCE (May) & Q1 GDP third estimate
catalysts.rationale: Core PCE is the Fed's preferred inflation gauge; a sticky reading at ~3.3% validates the hawkish dot plot and extends the dollar bid, pressuring Cable further. The data point is the most important near-term test of the Fed-hike thesis: if PCE surprises sticky, the hawkish repricing deepens; if it surprises soft on cheaper energy, it would be the first crack in the hike thesis and a Cable-positive relief. The Q1 GDP third estimate confirms or revises the US growth premium over the UK (~+1.5pp), feeding directly into the US-exceptionalism dollar narrative.

US Non-Farm Payrolls, released on the first Friday of each month, is the single biggest scheduled dollar mover. A firm print (above ~175k with contained wages) keeps the Fed hawkish and the dollar bid, extending Cable's slide; a soft print (below ~125k) reopens the cut narrative and provides Cable relief. The bilateral transmission is via the USD leg entirely — it is the purest test of US labour market resilience and the Fed's ability to maintain its hawkish stance heading into the July FOMC.

US CPI, released mid-month, is the second-biggest scheduled dollar mover. With headline at 4.2%, a still-hot core print keeps the Fed hawkish and the dollar bid; the first sign of energy-led headline cooling would provide Cable relief. The data point arrives two weeks before the July FOMC and is the decisive input into whether the Fed hikes — a hot reading would significantly raise July hike odds and drive Cable toward 1.3000, while a soft reading would be the most powerful near-term Cable-positive catalyst available.

A soft UK services-CPI print (below 3.5%) brings BoE cuts into view (Cable down via lower GBP carry and hawkish-hold erosion), while sticky services above 3.7% supports the hawkish hold (Cable up via preserved GBP relative carry). This is the key GBP-leg input ahead of the 30 July MPR — it determines whether the BoE can maintain its hawkish posture or must signal a dovish pivot. If services CPI softens while US CPI remains sticky, the bilateral rate path swings decisively toward the dollar.

The first meeting where the June dot-plot hike could materialise; even a hawkish hold sustains the dollar and caps Cable, while an actual hike would drive Cable toward 1.28. The Fed meets before the BoE, setting the broad-dollar tone that the BoE must respond to. A unanimous hawkish hold with retention of the hike bias in the statement is the baseline (Cable stays pressured); a delivered hike would be the most powerful near-term Cable-negative catalyst, breaking 1.3000.

A hold at 3.75% is expected, but the MPR scenario revisions and vote split set the BoE's direction. A retained hawkish hold with hawkish scenario revisions is Cable-positive (GBP carry preserved, bilateral rate balance maintained); a dovish mark-down on energy disinflation is Cable-negative (GBP loses its relative hawkishness). Sequenced after the FOMC, so the BoE response to the prior day's Fed decision determines whether the June bilateral rate divergence widens or narrows — the most important GBP-leg event in the tactical window.

A durable deal cements risk-on (mild Cable-positive via GBP cyclical, mild Cable-negative via USD haven unwind — roughly neutral); a breakdown restores the USD haven bid and Brent oil spike (Cable-negative, as USD haven bid plus GBP cyclical loss). The bilateral asymmetry is that a breakdown is more decisively Cable-negative than a continuation of the ceasefire is Cable-positive, given USD's dominant haven role versus GBP's modest cyclical profile. The market currently prices a durable outcome; the breakdown tail is under-priced.

The pivotal cross-horizon inflection — a fresh dot plot reveals whether the hike survives energy disinflation into autumn. A retained hawkish dot (hike still the median) keeps Cable pressured into Q4; a walk-back of the June dot (back to holds or cuts) is the most powerful strategic Cable-positive catalyst, softening the broad dollar. This is the meeting most likely to flip the pair from bearish to neutral/bullish, as it determines whether the tactical-to-strategic transition occurs earlier or later than expected. It is also the Fed's first opportunity to respond to the full post-war energy disinflation impulse.

A non-MPR hold likely, but any vote-split shift toward easing (as energy disinflation broadens) moves sterling. If the BoE begins signalling cuts while the Fed retains its hike bias, the bilateral rate-path divergence widens decisively in the dollar's favour — Cable-negative. Tone relative to the Fed and ECB sets the relative carry that will govern the pair through the autumn Budget season.

A government shutdown or messy continuing resolution injects Treasury-market and political volatility pre-midterms — mildly USD-negative on fiscal credibility concerns (Cable-positive), though a flight-to-quality response could reinforce USD haven bid (Cable-negative). The bilateral impact depends on whether the market reads a shutdown as a US-specific credit negative (mild Cable-positive) or a global risk-off trigger (Cable-negative). The timing — one month before midterms and two months before the UK Budget — makes this a potential compound volatility event.

Control of Congress affects the fiscal outlook and the dollar's medium-term trajectory — a binary political-risk node shaping the 2027 fiscal/Fed-pressure path. A Democratic sweep increases fiscal constraint (dollar-negative via lower deficit); a Republican sweep raises fiscal stimulus risk (dollar-positive near-term, then negative via deficit expansion). The bilateral impact on Cable is indirect via the USD leg and the 2027 Fed-pressure trajectory, occurring one month after the UK Budget creates a compound GBP/USD volatility window.

The dominant strategic UK event — thin fiscal headroom (<£10bn), debt ~95-96% of GDP, record tax burden, foreign-gilt reliance ~25-30% of stock, and gilt-Bund premium already at +183bps. A fragile, 2022-mini-budget-style plan triggers gilt stress and sharp GBP weakness (Cable sharply down); a credible package that rebuilds headroom compresses the gilt-Bund premium (Cable up). The bilateral asymmetry is decisive: there is no symmetric USD event as acutely dated and concentrated in its downside risk. The Autumn Budget is the single most important strategic inflection point for GBP/USD over the 1-6 month horizon.

Year-end Fed meeting and final 2026 dot plot, framing the 2027 cutting path that consensus expects will be dollar-bearish. A dovish 2027 dot (cuts pencilled in) would be a Cable-positive into year-end, validating the consensus recovery toward 1.36-1.40; retention of the hawkish stance would extend Cable's pressure into year-end. This meeting frames the strategic regime for H1 2027 and will determine whether the consensus year-end recovery is front-loaded or deferred.

invalidators.condition: GBP/USD breaks and holds below 1.3000 (52-week low / psychological pivot)
invalidators.new_stance: Confirms and deepens the Bearish stance; conviction increases to 4-5
invalidators.mechanism: A close below 1.3000 invalidates the multi-year uptrend from 1.0351 (2022 low) and triggers CTA and trend-following systematic selling that is specific to Cable — a technical regime change that adds a self-reinforcing bearish momentum layer on top of the fundamental drivers, accelerating the move toward 1.28-1.25.

invalidators.condition: Fed delivers a 25bp hike at the July or September FOMC while the BoE holds at 3.75%
invalidators.mechanism: A delivered Fed hike widens the rate-path divergence decisively in the dollar's favour, removes any residual GBP carry cushion (the +13bps gap becomes negative), validates the June dot plot, and likely triggers a repricing of 2027 Fed expectations — compounding the dollar bid and Cable downside. The GBP leveraged long flush would accelerate on such a move.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: FOMC decision statement and dot plot

invalidators.condition: UK Autumn Budget triggers a gilt-stress episode with gilt-Bund spread widening above 220bps and 30Y gilt yield above 5.75%
invalidators.mechanism: A fragile Budget triggers a 2022-mini-budget-style UK-specific GBP shock — gilt yields spike, foreign gilt buyers retreat, the Bank of England faces a financial stability mandate conflict, and sterling falls sharply independent of the dollar move. This is a GBP-weakness trade rather than a USD-strength trade, and it has no USD-leg equivalent to offset it bilaterally. Cable could approach 1.25-1.28 on such a scenario.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: UK gilt-Bund 10Y spread and 30Y gilt yield

invalidators.condition: US core PCE falls below 2.8% while the BoE retains its hawkish hold at the July MPR
invalidators.new_stance: Flips to Bullish; current Bearish stance invalidated
invalidators.mechanism: A soft PCE print evaporates the Fed-hike rationale (USD softens as rate-path reprices toward holds/cuts), while a hawkish BoE MPR preserves GBP's relative hawkishness — the bilateral rate-path swings back toward sterling, reversing the 'both-hawkish, dollar-dominance' regime. Cable would likely rally sharply toward 1.3428-1.3522 as the post-Fed break is reversed and dollar longs are squeezed.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: US core PCE (monthly) and BoE vote split/MPR guidance

invalidators.condition: Broad USD index (DXY) breaks decisively below 97 on a Fed dovish pivot
invalidators.new_stance: Flips to Bullish; Cable cap lifts
invalidators.mechanism: With Cable a pure dollar trade (GBP carry dead at +13bps), a decisive broad-dollar break removes the single dominant headwind pinning Cable at its lows. A DXY below 97 implies significant Fed pivot pricing that would reprice the forward curve sharply GBP-positive, lift the rate-path ceiling for Cable, and reverse the 17 June range break — opening a recovery toward 1.38-1.40 as the consensus recovery thesis plays out.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: DXY spot and DTWEXBGS broad trade-weighted USD index

invalidators.condition: EUR/GBP breaks above 0.8750 while GBP/USD also stays soft
invalidators.new_stance: Deepens Bearish conviction — signals GBP weakness rather than pure dollar strength
invalidators.mechanism: If EUR/GBP rises above 0.8750 simultaneously with Cable staying soft, it signals GBP is underperforming against both EUR and USD — a genuine sterling-weakness story rather than the 'both-hawkish, dollar-dominance' bilateral thesis. This would require revisiting all the neutral bilateral factor assessments (risk, energy, external balance) that rely on the dollar-trade framing, and would likely imply a larger Cable downside than the current 1.3000 target.

signposts.current_level: ~66% odds of at least 1 hike in 2026
signposts.confirms_if: Rises above 75% — Fed hawkishness intensifies, dollar bid extends, Cable grinds toward 1.3000
signposts.invalidates_if: Falls below 40% — market reprices toward holds/cuts, dollar softens, Cable rallies toward 1.3428+
signposts.bilateral_importance: This is the single most important Cable driver given the dead-carry regime (+13bps GBP-USD). With GBP carry providing no buffer, the pair is governed by the Fed's hike trajectory more than any UK factor — the Fed hike probability is the closest real-time proxy for the bilateral rate-path differential that drives ~40% of the weighted score.

signposts.indicator: GBP/USD spot vs 52-week low (1.30112) and MA cluster (1.341-1.345)
signposts.current_level: 1.3232 — below all MAs (50d 1.34354, 100d 1.34138, 200d 1.34458), 26th percentile of 52w range
signposts.confirms_if: Breaks and holds below 1.3000 — multi-year structure break, triggers trend-following selling
signposts.invalidates_if: Reclaims and holds above 1.3428 — back above MA cluster and broken support, invalidates the bearish break
signposts.bilateral_importance: The 1.3000 level is the make-or-break pivot for the multi-year Cable uptrend from 1.0351; a break is a regime-defining bilateral signal that the dollar-dominance trade has structural legs beyond the tactical Fed-repricing catalyst, potentially opening the 1.28-1.25 zone. Conversely, a reclaim of 1.3428 would signal the 17 June break was a false break, invalidating the bearish setup.

signposts.confirms_if: DXY holds above 100 or breaks above 102 — firm dollar cap on Cable maintained
signposts.invalidates_if: DXY breaks below 97 — Fed pivot pricing softens the broad dollar and lifts the Cable cap
signposts.bilateral_importance: The DXY climbed to a two-month high near 100.57 following the hawkish Fed, breaking above key resistance with momentum. The firm dollar is the dominant force on Cable since GBP carry provides no offset at +13bps — the broad dollar is the single most important macro variable for the pair on a daily basis, determining whether the tactical bearish thesis extends or reverses.

signposts.confirms_if: Widens above 220bps — UK fiscal-risk repricing accelerates, GBP-negative independent of dollar
signposts.invalidates_if: Compresses inside 150bps — fiscal reassurance reduces UK risk premium, GBP-positive
signposts.bilateral_importance: The gilt-Bund spread is the clearest gauge of UK-specific fiscal risk that can decouple Cable from the broad-dollar narrative — it is the primary transmission channel through which the Autumn Budget affects sterling independent of the USD leg. A widening spread signals the 'kindness of strangers' financing fragility is materialising; a compression signals the fiscal tail is being de-risked. This is the most important strategic signpost for the bilateral analysis.

signposts.confirms_if: EUR/GBP falls below 0.8500 while Cable stays soft — confirms GBP is strong vs EUR but weak vs USD, validating the dollar-trade thesis
signposts.invalidates_if: EUR/GBP rises above 0.8750 — GBP weak against both EUR and USD, signals genuine sterling weakness rather than pure dollar strength
signposts.bilateral_importance: EUR/GBP is the critical cross-check on whether Cable's decline is a USD-strength or GBP-weakness story. If EUR/GBP holds or falls while Cable drops, it confirms the bilateral insight that Cable is a dollar trade and that GBP exposure is better expressed via EUR/GBP. If EUR/GBP rises, it signals a fundamental GBP problem that would require revising all the neutral bilateral assessments in this analysis.

signposts.confirms_if: Stays above 3.5% — BoE hawkish hold sustained, GBP relative hawkishness preserved
signposts.invalidates_if: Falls below 3.0% — energy disinflation broadens to services, BoE dovish pivot comes into view, GBP loses its residual hawkish support
signposts.bilateral_importance: UK services CPI is the key gauge of whether the BoE can preserve its hawkish hold relative to a softening Fed — the GBP-leg input that determines whether the bilateral rate-path divergence narrows back toward sterling. It is also the primary indicator of whether the UK's structural services-inflation stickiness (3.7% vs US supercore 3.7%) will prove durable enough to offset the UK's growth disadvantage over the strategic horizon.

assumptions: Price data (spot 1.3232, 52-week range 1.30112-1.38621, range percentile 26%, MAs 50d 1.34354/100d 1.34138/200d 1.34458) is sourced from the OHLC series and is the source of truth, superseding the mid-range (1.3430) framing of the standalone dossiers which were priced before the full 17 June break was complete.

Bilateral carry (+13bps) is computed from the BoE Bank Rate of 3.75% and the Fed mid-rate of 3.625%; one secondary source cited a BoE rate of 4.00% which was not adopted — the 3.75% figure from the base dossier is used throughout.
Forward points (1M +3 pips, 3M +9 pips, 6M +18 pips) are directional approximations derived from the +13bps carry differential and are not live forward feed prices.
Support and resistance levels are web-sourced from technical analysis providers (forex.com, FXStreet, ActionForex) as visual chart analysis is not derivable from raw OHLC data.

Implied volatility (1M 7.2%, 3M 7.6%, IV percentile ~40th, RV 5.5%), risk reversals (-0.3 vols GBP puts bid), and variance premium assessments are dossier-estimated and calibrated to the pre-break regime; post-break realised vol will have risen, compressing the variance premium.

The key analytical choice is treating Cable as a 'both-hawkish, dollar-dominance' trade rather than a GBP-weakness trade. Energy/disinflation/risk channels that score GBP-positive in isolation are neutralised bilaterally because they apply symmetrically or more strongly to the USD leg — driving the Neutral scores on Factors 3, 5 and 7 where the standalone GBP dossier was Bullish.
Bilateral trade data uses IMF DOTS figures directly (UK net goods surplus vs US $1.0bn for 2024) — a near-balanced relationship generating no structural pair flow.

Default factor weights applied throughout; no pair-specific adjustments were made. While Cable's dead carry might argue for trimming Factor 1's weight, the relative-rate-path (rather than carry level) remains the dominant driver, so the default 0.24 is retained.
Stance scoring maps Bullish to +1, Neutral to 0, Bearish to -1, scaled by confidence/100 multiplied by weight.
The USD dossier's June core PCE figure (3.3% April) predates the 25 June print; the analysis uses pre-25-June data and the PCE release is identified as a key upcoming catalyst.
Both standalone dossiers flag the GBP/USD cross-currency basis as a data gap; forward points are therefore approximated from the carry differential rather than live basis swap quotes.
The Autumn Budget date is estimated as approximately late November 2026 based on typical UK Budget timing; the precise date had not been confirmed at the time of analysis.
CFTC positioning data referenced is as of 9 June 2026 — there is a typical one-week lag in CFTC reporting, so positioning may have shifted post-17-June break.
